pattern: Sort by cost so the budget cutoff becomes a shrinking prefix, then track the best and second-best capacity in that prefix to pair with any right-hand item in O(n log n).
hints: You may buy zero, one, or two machines - the cost pair must be strictly under budget. What's the brute-force pairing cost, and how would sorting by cost help shrink the search?
After sorting by cost ascending, for a fixed machine the set of partners it can afford is a prefix of the sorted order. What do you need to know about that prefix to answer instantly?

Keep a running best and second-best capacity over the cost-sorted prefix (with their positions). For each machine, binary-search the largest affordable prefix and combine with whichever of best/second-best isn't the machine itself.

edge_inputs.description: budget too small for any purchase
pairing beats the single best-capacity machine
tied costs must still combine two distinct machines
cheapest pair is not the two cheapest by cost alone

stress.description: 10k random machines near the cost ceiling to stress the prefix binary search
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    costs = [rng.randint(1, 100000) for _ in range(n)]
    capacity = [rng.randint(1, 100000) for _ in range(n)]
    budget = rng.randint(1, 200000)
    return [costs, capacity, budget]
